Fix REAL defects in a generated LLD session, then re-verify by running tests. Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/b9b8118c-3795-43c8-b3ac-5bf4a6119a42_lld/

The adversarial reviewer found these actionable issues — fix EACH in the CODE and/or _response.md (keep them consistent — if you change code shown in a §6 ```python block, update that block too):
1. [MAJOR] exchange_service.py:118-134 (snapshot) and :103-116 (get_order); root cause models.py:168-175 (_peek_active heappop): READ PATH BYPASSES THE LOCK WHILE MUTATING THE HEAP. snapshot() and get_order() run OUTSIDE self._lock(symbol), but snapshot() calls best_bid()/best_ask()/is_crossed() -> _peek_active(), which does heapq.heappop() to discard lazily-deleted dead tops. heappop is a MUTATION. A monitoring/dashboard thread calling snapshot() concurrently with a submit_order() on the SAME symbol has two threads mutating the same heap list unsynchronized. Reproduced under contention: IndexError: index out of range, traceback ending at models.py:173 `heapq.heappop(heap)` via exchange_service.py:126 `ask = book.best_ask()`. This is the canonical concurrency use case (poll book state while orders flow), and it tears the book / crashes — directly violating the design's headline claim that the per-symbol lock keeps the book consistent. The very method marketed as 'proves the book isn't crossed' is itself unsafe under the contention the design promises to handle. (get_order is a pure dict.get and is safe; only snapshot is exploitable.)
   FIX: Acquire the per-symbol lock in snapshot() (and any read path that triggers _peek_active): wrap the book reads in `with self._lock(symbol):`. Alternatively make peeks non-mutating (separate the lazy-purge from the read, or purge only inside locked write operations) so reads never call heappop.
2. [MAJOR] tests/test_exchange.py:199-243 (test_concurrent_crossing_conserves_quantity) and _response.md:1101, 1227-1255: CONCURRENCY TEST DOES NOT PROVE THE LOCK IS LOAD-BEARING. The Barrier(50) test asserts conservation + uniqueness + committed state (good structure), but it passes identically whether the per-symbol lock is present or not: running the same scenario on plain MatchingEngine (no-op _lock) passes 30/30 even with sys.setswitchinterval(1e-6) and 80x25 contention. The race (lost update on the resting buy's remaining_qty -= qty) only manifests when a yield is artificially injected into Order.fill (then 8/15 trials corrupt). Under real CPython the GIL plus C-level heapq/dict ops keep the read-modify-write window closed, so a serialised/unlocked run is indistinguishable from the locked run. The §7.2 claim 'without the per-symbol lock, 400 shares traded while the resting buy showed 390 remaining' is NOT reproducible with the actual code. The test therefore demonstrates conservation-in-practice-on-CPython, not that the lock prevents anything, and no test exercises the genuinely-broken snapshot/match read-write race.
   FIX: Strengthen the test so it actually distinguishes locked from unlocked: e.g. assert it FAILS on plain MatchingEngine (a negative control), and/or add a test that runs snapshot() concurrently with submit_order() on one symbol and asserts no exception/torn state (this currently fails). Optionally widen the race window deterministically (inject a yield via a test hook) to prove the lock closes it. Correct or remove the §7.2 'I verified the race is real / 390 remaining' narrative since the shipped code+test does not exhibit it.

Rules: fix the CODE not the test (unless the test itself is too weak to prove the invariant — then strengthen it). Keep Python 3.11+ idioms, one domain exception base, no bare except, integer cents for money, no '...' bodies, slow I/O outside locks. If you touch the §8 sequence diagram, ensure NO semicolons in message text. After fixing, run `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/b9b8118c-3795-43c8-b3ac-5bf4a6119a42_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q` and confirm both green. Update §7.1/§7.2 if the test count/output changed. Return what you changed and final test status.

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain model for the order matching engine.
3	
4	The "nouns" of the exchange live here: enums, the typed exception family,
5	the immutable Trade record, the mutable Order entity, and the OrderBook for
6	ONE symbol (the Information Expert that owns price-time matching via two heaps).
7	"""
8	
9	import heapq
10	import itertools
11	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
12	from enum import Enum
13	
14	
15	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
16	# A trader either wants to BUY or SELL. This is the single most important field
17	# on an order — it decides which side of the book the order rests on and which
18	# side it can match against. We NEVER collapse it into a bool; a named side is
19	# what the interviewer wants to see survive end to end.
20	# 🎙️ "Side is BUY or SELL. A buy can only fill against asks, a sell against bids."
21	class Side(Enum):
22	    BUY = "BUY"
23	    SELL = "SELL"
24	
25	
26	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
27	# An order moves through a tiny lifecycle. We mark it explicitly so a caller can
28	# ask "did my order rest, partially fill, fully fill, or get cancelled?" without
29	# re-deriving it from quantities.
30	class OrderStatus(Enum):
31	    OPEN = "OPEN"                  # resting on the book, nothing filled yet
32	    PARTIALLY_FILLED = "PARTIAL"   # some quantity filled, remainder resting
33	    FILLED = "FILLED"              # fully filled, no longer on the book
34	    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"        # cancelled by the trader, remainder removed
35	
36	
37	# ── WHY ONE BASE EXCEPTION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
38	# One base class for every failure this domain can raise. Callers can catch
39	# ExchangeError to handle "anything the engine rejected" and still pattern-match
40	# on the specific subclass. We NEVER raise a bare ValueError/RuntimeError from
41	# our own code — a typed error tells the caller exactly what went wrong.
42	class ExchangeError(Exception):
43	    """Base class for every error the matching engine raises."""
44	
45	
46	class UnknownSymbolError(ExchangeError):
47	    """A symbol was referenced that was never registered on the engine."""
48	
49	
50	class InvalidOrderError(ExchangeError):
51	    """An order failed validation — bad price, bad quantity, or bad side."""
52	
53	
54	class UnknownOrderError(ExchangeError):
55	    """A cancel referenced an order id the engine has never seen."""
56	
57	
58	class OrderNotCancellableError(ExchangeError):
59	    """A cancel hit an order that is already filled or already cancelled."""
60	
61	
62	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
63	# A Trade is the FACT that a buy and a sell met: who, how much, at what price.
64	# It never changes once printed, so it is frozen — once a trade is recorded the
65	# exchange has made a promise it cannot edit. Price is integer CENTS, never a
66	# float, because money rounding errors are unacceptable on an exchange.
67	# 🎙️ "A Trade is immutable — once two orders match, that print is permanent."
68	@dataclass(frozen=True)
69	class Trade:
70	    trade_id: str
71	    symbol: str
72	    buy_order_id: str
73	    sell_order_id: str
74	    price_cents: int               # the resting order's price sets the trade price
75	    quantity: int                  # shares exchanged in THIS fill (may be partial)
76	
77	
78	# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
79	# An Order is the live thing on the book. It owns its OWN remaining quantity and
80	# its OWN status — the book asks it to fill, it does the arithmetic on itself.
81	# It is mutable because remaining_qty shrinks as it fills; that is its whole job.
82	# 🎙️ "The Order tracks how much of it is left. When I fill it, IT decrements
83	#      its own remaining and flips its own status — no one reaches in and edits it."
84	@dataclass
85	class Order:
86	    order_id: str
87	    symbol: str
88	    side: Side
89	    price_cents: int               # limit price — integer cents
90	    quantity: int                  # original requested quantity (never changes)
91	    sequence: int                  # arrival rank — the TIME in price-time priority
92	    remaining_qty: int = field(init=False)
93	    status: OrderStatus = field(default=OrderStatus.OPEN, init=False)
94	
95	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
96	        # remaining starts equal to the full order; it only ever shrinks.
97	        self.remaining_qty = self.quantity
98	
99	    @property
100	    def filled_qty(self) -> int:
101	        # How much has been filled so far. Derived, never stored twice.
102	        return self.quantity - self.remaining_qty
103	
104	    @property
105	    def is_active(self) -> bool:
106	        # Active means it still has shares that could match or rest.
107	        return self.status in (OrderStatus.OPEN, OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED)
108	
109	    def fill(self, qty: int) -> None:
110	        # WHY: the order mutates ITSELF when filled. The book computes how many
111	        #      shares trade, then tells the order "you lost this many". The order
112	        #      guards its own invariant: you can never fill more than remains.
113	        if qty <= 0 or qty > self.remaining_qty:
114	            raise InvalidOrderError(
115	                f"cannot fill {qty} of order {self.order_id} "
116	                f"with {self.remaining_qty} remaining"
117	            )
118	        self.remaining_qty -= qty
119	
120	        if self.remaining_qty == 0:
121	            self.status = OrderStatus.FILLED
122	        else:
123	            self.status = OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
124	
125	    def cancel(self) -> None:
126	        # WHY: cancelling just flips status. The book stops handing the order out
127	        #      once it is no longer active. We do NOT physically rip it out of the
128	        #      heap here — that is O(n); instead matching skips cancelled orders
129	        #      lazily when they reach the top. This keeps cancel O(1).
130	        self.status = OrderStatus.CANCELLED
131	
132	
133	# ── WHY THIS HELPER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
134	# A heap of orders needs a strict ordering key. We never put the Order object
135	# itself on the heap (two orders are never "less than" each other); instead we
136	# push a tuple whose first elements ARE the price-time priority, and carry the
137	# order as payload. _HeapEntry makes that tuple comparison explicit and total.
138	@dataclass(order=True)
139	class _HeapEntry:
140	    sort_key: tuple[int, int]      # (price component, sequence) — compared first
141	    order: Order = field(compare=False)   # payload, never compared
142	
143	
144	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE INFORMATION EXPERT) ──────────────────────────────────
145	# The OrderBook owns ALL matching for ONE symbol. It is the Information Expert:
146	# it holds both sides, so it is the only thing that can decide whether two orders
147	# cross and in what order. The engine never reaches inside the book to match —
148	# it hands the book a new order and the book returns the trades.
149	#
150	# Two heaps give price-time priority in O(log n):
151	#   - bids: a MAX-heap on price (highest buyer first). Python heapq is a MIN-heap,
152	#     so we store -price to flip it.
153	#   - asks: a MIN-heap on price (lowest seller first) — heapq's natural order.
154	# Within one price, the heap's second key (sequence) breaks ties by EARLIEST
155	# arrival — that is the "time" in price-time priority, and it is FIFO.
156	# 🎙️ "The book is two heaps. Best bid is the highest price; best ask is the
157	#      lowest price. They cross when the best bid is at least the best ask."
158	class OrderBook:
159	    def __init__(self, symbol: str) -> None:
160	        self.symbol = symbol
161	        self._bids: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # max-heap by price (store -price)
162	        self._asks: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # min-heap by price
163	        # id → Order, so cancel can find an order and flip its status in O(1).
164	        self._orders_by_id: dict[str, Order] = {}
165	
166	    # ── best-of-book peeks (skip lazily-deleted dead orders) ──────────────────
167	
168	    def _peek_active(self, heap: list[_HeapEntry]) -> Order | None:
169	        # WHY: cancelled or fully-filled orders are left in the heap (lazy delete)
170	        #      and popped off the top only when we look. We discard dead tops here
171	        #      so the caller always sees a live best order or None.
172	        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
173	            heapq.heappop(heap)
174	
175	        return heap[0].order if heap else None
176	
177	    def best_bid(self) -> Order | None:
178	        return self._peek_active(self._bids)
179	
180	    def best_ask(self) -> Order | None:
181	        return self._peek_active(self._asks)
182	
183	    # ── matching (the heart) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
184	
185	    def match(self, incoming: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]") -> list[Trade]:
186	        # WHY: a SINGLE incoming order may sweep MANY resting orders on the other
187	        #      side, generating several trades, then rest its remainder. All of
188	        #      that happens here in one call so the book never tears — the engine
189	        #      runs this whole method under one per-symbol lock.
190	        self._orders_by_id[incoming.order_id] = incoming
191	        trades: list[Trade] = []
192	
193	        opposite = self._asks if incoming.side is Side.BUY else self._bids
194	
195	        while incoming.remaining_qty > 0:
196	            resting = self._peek_active(opposite)
197	
198	            if resting is None or not self._crosses(incoming, resting):
199	                break
200	
201	            trades.append(self._execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids))
202	
203	        if incoming.remaining_qty > 0 and incoming.is_active:
204	            self._rest(incoming)
205	
206	        return trades
207	
208	    def _crosses(self, incoming: Order, resting: Order) -> bool:
209	        # WHY: a buy crosses a sell when the buyer will pay AT LEAST what the
210	        #      seller asks. A sell crosses a bid when the seller accepts AT MOST
211	        #      what the buyer offers. This is the "limit" in limit order.
212	        if incoming.side is Side.BUY:
213	            return incoming.price_cents >= resting.price_cents
214	
215	        return incoming.price_cents <= resting.price_cents
216	
217	    def _execute(
218	        self, incoming: Order, resting: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]"
219	    ) -> Trade:
220	        # WHY: the fill quantity is the smaller of the two remaining sizes — that
221	        #      is what makes a fill PARTIAL. The trade prints at the RESTING
222	        #      order's price (price-time priority: the order that waited sets the
223	        #      price, so the aggressor never gets a worse price than they asked).
224	        qty = min(incoming.remaining_qty, resting.remaining_qty)
225	        price = resting.price_cents
226	
227	        incoming.fill(qty)
228	        resting.fill(qty)
229	
230	        buy_id = incoming.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else resting.order_id
231	        sell_id = resting.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else incoming.order_id
232	
233	        return Trade(
234	            trade_id=f"trade-{next(trade_ids)}",
235	            symbol=self.symbol,
236	            buy_order_id=buy_id,
237	            sell_order_id=sell_id,
238	            price_cents=price,
239	            quantity=qty,
240	        )
241	
242	    def _rest(self, order: Order) -> None:
243	        # WHY: whatever did not match goes onto the book to wait. The sort key is
244	        #      (price, sequence). For bids we negate price so the MAX price sits
245	        #      at the heap top; for asks the natural MIN price sits on top. The
246	        #      sequence is the tie-breaker = earliest arrival wins at one price.
247	        if order.side is Side.BUY:
248	            key = (-order.price_cents, order.sequence)
249	            heapq.heappush(self._bids, _HeapEntry(key, order))
250	        else:
251	            key = (order.price_cents, order.sequence)
252	            heapq.heappush(self._asks, _HeapEntry(key, order))
253	
254	    # ── cancel ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
255	
256	    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
257	        # WHY: look the order up by id, then ask IT to cancel itself. We do not
258	        #      remove it from the heap now (O(n)); matching skips it lazily.
259	        order = self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
260	
261	        if order is None:
262	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found on {self.symbol}")
263	
264	        if not order.is_active:
265	            raise OrderNotCancellableError(
266	                f"order {order_id} is {order.status.value}, cannot cancel"
267	            )
268	
269	        order.cancel()
270	        return order
271	
272	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order | None:
273	        return self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
274	
275	    # ── invariant checks (used by tests and the demo) ─────────────────────────
276	
277	    def is_crossed(self) -> bool:
278	        # WHY: a healthy book is NEVER crossed — the best bid must be strictly
279	        #      below the best ask after matching settles. If best_bid >= best_ask
280	        #      we left money on the table (two orders that should have traded did
281	        #      not). The concurrency test asserts this is False.
282	        bid = self.best_bid()
283	        ask = self.best_ask()
284	
285	        if bid is None or ask is None:
286	            return False
287	
288	        return bid.price_cents >= ask.price_cents
289	
290	    def resting_quantity(self, side: Side) -> int:
291	        # WHY: total live shares resting on one side. Used to prove conservation:
292	        #      shares submitted == shares filled + shares still resting.
293	        heap = self._bids if side is Side.BUY else self._asks
294	        return sum(e.order.remaining_qty for e in heap if e.order.is_active)
295

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The matching engine orchestrator.
3	
4	`MatchingEngine` is the clean, single-threaded core. It owns one OrderBook per
5	symbol and a global id->symbol index so a cancel can find any order. The public
6	methods (`submit_order`, `cancel_order`, `get_order`, `snapshot`) are the entire
7	external surface.
8	
9	Concurrency is a deliberate SECOND version: `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` overrides
10	ONLY the `_lock` hook to take a PER-SYMBOL reentrant lock, so different symbols
11	match in parallel while everything touching ONE book serialises. The core file
12	below has zero lock code.
13	"""
14	
15	import itertools
16	import threading
17	from collections.abc import Iterator
18	from contextlib import contextmanager
19	
20	from models import (
21	    ExchangeError,
22	    InvalidOrderError,
23	    Order,
24	    OrderBook,
25	    Side,
26	    Trade,
27	    UnknownOrderError,
28	    UnknownSymbolError,
29	)
30	
31	
32	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE ORCHESTRATOR) ────────────────────────────────────────
33	# The engine is the public API. It does NOT match orders itself — it routes an
34	# incoming order to the right symbol's OrderBook (Information Expert) and returns
35	# the trades. It owns the symbol->book map, the id->symbol index for cancels, and
36	# the monotonic counters that stamp arrival sequence (the "time" in price-time).
37	# 🎙️ "The engine is a thin router. The book does the matching; the engine just
38	#      finds the right book, gives it the order, and hands back the trades."
39	class MatchingEngine:
40	    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
41	        if not symbols:
42	            raise InvalidOrderError("engine needs at least one symbol")
43	
44	        # symbol -> its own independent book. Different symbols share NO state,
45	        # which is exactly why the thread-safe version can lock per symbol.
46	        self._books: dict[str, OrderBook] = {s: OrderBook(s) for s in symbols}
47	        # order id -> symbol, so cancel_order/get_order can route without the
48	        # caller telling us the symbol again.
49	        self._order_symbol: dict[str, str] = {}
50	        # Numbered ids make test assertions readable; UUIDs in production.
51	        # One counter stamps BOTH the order id and its arrival sequence so the
52	        # id "ord-7" and sequence 7 always agree — easy to read in tests.
53	        self._order_seq = itertools.count(1)
54	        self._trade_seq = itertools.count(1)
55	
56	    # ── concurrency hook (no-op in the clean core) ───────────────────────────
57	
58	    @contextmanager
59	    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
60	        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there is zero lock code in
61	        #      this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method with
62	        #      a real per-symbol lock. The diff between clean and safe is ~10 lines.
63	        yield
64	
65	    # ── public surface ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
66	
67	    def submit_order(
68	        self, symbol: str, side: Side, price_cents: int, quantity: int
69	    ) -> tuple[Order, list[Trade]]:
70	        # WHY: this is the pipeline — validate, build the order, match it against
71	        #      the book, rest the remainder. The whole match runs under one
72	        #      per-symbol lock so a single submit either fully resolves or not at
73	        #      all; the book never tears mid-sweep.
74	        self._validate(symbol, price_cents, quantity)
75	        book = self._books[symbol]
76	
77	        with self._lock(symbol):
78	            seq = next(self._order_seq)
79	            order = Order(
80	                order_id=f"ord-{seq}",
81	                symbol=symbol,
82	                side=side,
83	                price_cents=price_cents,
84	                quantity=quantity,
85	                sequence=seq,
86	            )
87	            self._order_symbol[order.order_id] = symbol
88	            trades = book.match(order, self._trade_seq)
89	
90	        return order, trades
91	
92	    def cancel_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
93	        # WHY: route by the id index to the owning book, then cancel under that
94	        #      book's lock so a cancel can never race a match on the same symbol.
95	        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)
96	
97	        if symbol is None:
98	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
99	
100	        with self._lock(symbol):
101	            return self._books[symbol].cancel(order_id)
102	
103	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
104	        # WHY: read the live order so a caller can inspect fill state. Routed via
105	        #      the same id index. A missing id is a typed error, not a None.
106	        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)
107	
108	        if symbol is None:
109	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
110	
111	        order = self._books[symbol].get_order(order_id)
112	
113	        if order is None:
114	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")
115	
116	        return order
117	
118	    def snapshot(self, symbol: str) -> dict[str, int | bool | None]:
119	        # WHY: a read-only view of one book's top of book + resting depth. Used by
120	        #      the demo and tests to prove the book is consistent (not crossed).
121	        if symbol not in self._books:
122	            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")
123	
124	        book = self._books[symbol]
125	        bid = book.best_bid()
126	        ask = book.best_ask()
127	
128	        return {
129	            "best_bid": bid.price_cents if bid else None,
130	            "best_ask": ask.price_cents if ask else None,
131	            "bid_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.BUY),
132	            "ask_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.SELL),
133	            "crossed": book.is_crossed(),
134	        }
135	
136	    # ── validation helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
137	
138	    def _validate(self, symbol: str, price_cents: int, quantity: int) -> None:
139	        # WHY: reject bad input at the public boundary BEFORE we mutate any book.
140	        #      Every guard raises a specific typed error so the caller knows why.
141	        if symbol not in self._books:
142	            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")
143	
144	        if price_cents <= 0:
145	            raise InvalidOrderError(f"price must be positive, got {price_cents}")
146	
147	        if quantity <= 0:
148	            raise InvalidOrderError(f"quantity must be positive, got {quantity}")
149	
150	
151	# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
152	# The thread-safe version. It overrides ONLY the `_lock` hook — every public
153	# method above is inherited UNCHANGED. We keep one RLock PER SYMBOL so two threads
154	# trading different symbols never block each other; only threads hitting the SAME
155	# symbol serialise. That is the whole concurrency design in one method.
156	# 🎙️ "Thread-safety is a 10-line subclass. One reentrant lock per symbol. Orders
157	#      on different symbols match in parallel; same-symbol orders take turns."
158	class ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(MatchingEngine):
159	    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
160	        super().__init__(symbols)
161	        # One lock per symbol. We build them up front (symbols are fixed at
162	        # construction) so there is no map-mutation race; if symbols could be
163	        # added at runtime we would guard this dict with its own lock too.
164	        self._symbol_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {
165	            s: threading.RLock() for s in self._books
166	        }
167	
168	    @contextmanager
169	    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
170	        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) not Lock — a future guarded method might call
171	        #      another guarded method on the same symbol; an RLock lets the same
172	        #      thread re-enter instead of deadlocking on itself. Per symbol, not
173	        #      global, so AAPL and MSFT match at the same time.
174	        lock = self._symbol_locks.get(symbol)
175	
176	        if lock is None:
177	            # Defensive: an unknown symbol should already have been rejected by
178	            # _validate, but if it slips through we fail loud, not silently.
179	            raise ExchangeError(f"no lock for unknown symbol {symbol}")
180	
181	        with lock:
182	            yield
183

2	"""Narrated demo driver for the order matching engine.

6	Cases cover: a simple cross, a partial fill leaving a remainder resting,

8	and a tiny concurrency proof that the book stays consistent.
9	"""

24	def case_1_simple_cross() -> None:
25	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
26	    # A seller rests 100 @ $10.00, then a buyer crosses at $10.00.
27	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
28	    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
29

31	    assert trades[0].quantity == 100
32	    assert trades[0].price_cents == 1000          # prints at the resting price
33	    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.FILLED
34	    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
35	    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0
36	    assert snap["crossed"] is False
37	    print("Case 1 simple full cross at resting price -> OK")
38	
39

41	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
42	    # Seller has only 60; buyer wants 100 -> 60 trade, 40 of the buy rests.
43	    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)
44	    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
45	
46	    assert len(trades) == 1 and trades[0].quantity == 60
47	    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
48	    assert buy.remaining_qty == 40
49	    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
50	    assert snap["best_bid"] == 1000 and snap["bid_qty"] == 40   # remainder rests
51	    assert snap["ask_qty"] == 0
52	    print("Case 2 partial fill leaves remainder resting -> OK")

55	def case_3_price_time_priority() -> None:
56	    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
57	    # Two sells at the SAME price; the EARLIER one must fill first (FIFO time).
58	    s_first, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
59	    s_second, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
60	    # A better-priced (lower) sell jumps the queue on PRICE.
61	    s_cheap, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=999, quantity=30)
62	
63	    _, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)
64	
65	    # Price first: the 999 sell (30) fills entirely, then the earliest 1000 sell.
66	    assert trades[0].sell_order_id == s_cheap.order_id and trades[0].quantity == 30
67	    assert trades[1].sell_order_id == s_first.order_id and trades[1].quantity == 30
68	    assert engine.get_order(s_first.order_id).remaining_qty == 20

792	### `OrderBook` — two heaps + match (the heart)
793	
794	The private comparison wrapper so heapq has a total order, then the book.
795	
796	```python copy
797	@dataclass(order=True)
798	class _HeapEntry:
799	    sort_key: tuple[int, int]              # (price component, sequence)
800	    order: Order = field(compare=False)    # payload, never compared
801	```
802	
803	> 🎙️ "I never put two Orders on a heap directly — one order isn't 'less than' another. I push a tuple `(price, sequence)` and carry the order as payload that's excluded from comparison."
804	
805	```python copy
806	class OrderBook:
807	    def __init__(self, symbol: str) -> None:
808	        self.symbol = symbol
809	        self._bids: list[_HeapEntry] = []      # max-heap by price (store -price)
810	        self._asks: list[_HeapEntry] = []      # min-heap by price
811	        self._orders_by_id: dict[str, Order] = {}
812	
813	    def _peek_active(self, heap: list[_HeapEntry]) -> Order | None:
814	        # WHY: cancelled/filled orders are left in the heap (lazy delete) and
815	        #      discarded only when they reach the top. Caller always sees a live best.
816	        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
817	            heapq.heappop(heap)
818	
819	        return heap[0].order if heap else None
820	
821	    def best_bid(self) -> Order | None:
822	        return self._peek_active(self._bids)
823	
824	    def best_ask(self) -> Order | None:
825	        return self._peek_active(self._asks)
826	```
827	
828	**`match` — role:** the one method that owns price-time priority and partial fills for this symbol.
829	
830	Core logic:
831	1. Register the incoming order by id
832	2. Repeatedly take the best opposite order
833	3. If none or it doesn't cross, stop
834	4. Trade `min(remainings)` at the resting price
835	5. Rest any remainder
836	
837	Edge cases: opposite side empty; best opposite doesn't cross; incoming fully filled mid-sweep; incoming partially filled then rests.
838	
839	```python copy
840	    def match(self, incoming: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]") -> list[Trade]:
841	        # WHY: one incoming order may sweep MANY resting orders and then rest its
842	        #      remainder — all here, so the engine can wrap the whole thing in one
843	        #      per-symbol lock and the book is never observed half-swept.
844	        self._orders_by_id[incoming.order_id] = incoming
845	        trades: list[Trade] = []
846	        opposite = self._asks if incoming.side is Side.BUY else self._bids
847	
848	        while incoming.remaining_qty > 0:
849	            resting = self._peek_active(opposite)
850	
851	            if resting is None or not self._crosses(incoming, resting):
852	                break
853	
854	            trades.append(self._execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids))
855	
856	        if incoming.remaining_qty > 0 and incoming.is_active:
857	            self._rest(incoming)
858	
859	        return trades
860	```
861	
862	> 🎙️ **Say (pipeline):** "Sweep the opposite side while the incoming still has shares AND the best opposite order crosses. Each pass prints one trade. Whatever's left rests."
863	> ⚠️ "What if two submits hit the same symbol at once?" → "That's the race I flagged. The engine wraps this whole `match` in a per-symbol lock, so a submit is all-or-nothing — no one sees the book half-swept."
864	
865	```python copy
866	    def _crosses(self, incoming: Order, resting: Order) -> bool:
867	        # WHY: a buy crosses when it'll pay AT LEAST the ask; a sell crosses when
868	        #      it accepts AT MOST the bid. This is the 'limit' in limit order.
869	        if incoming.side is Side.BUY:
870	            return incoming.price_cents >= resting.price_cents
871	        return incoming.price_cents <= resting.price_cents
872	```
873	
874	#### Approach — `_execute` (the most interesting helper: what price, what quantity?)
875	
876	**Approach 1 — fill at the incoming order's price (rejected):**
877	```
878	qty = min(incoming.remaining, resting.remaining)
879	trade_price = incoming.price_cents
880	```
881	> **Challenges:** A buy at $10.05 hitting a resting ask at $10.00 would print at $10.05 — the buyer overpays and the passive order's price is ignored. This breaks the price-time-priority promise; the passive order set the price first.
882	
883	**Approach 2 — split a partial fill into two trades to "even it out" (rejected for this scale):**
884	```
885	half = qty // 2
886	emit Trade(half) at resting; emit Trade(qty-half) at incoming
887	```
888	> **Challenges:** Invents trades that never happened — one fill is one trade. Doubles trade volume and confuses downstream settlement. Pure over-engineering.
889	
890	**Chosen — fill `min(remainings)` at the RESTING price, one trade per fill:**
891	```python copy
892	    def _execute(self, incoming, resting, trade_ids):
893	        qty = min(incoming.remaining_qty, resting.remaining_qty)   # partial-fill size
894	        price = resting.price_cents                                # passive price wins
895	        incoming.fill(qty)
896	        resting.fill(qty)
897	        buy_id = incoming.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else resting.order_id
898	        sell_id = resting.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else incoming.order_id
899	        return Trade(f"trade-{next(trade_ids)}", self.symbol, buy_id, sell_id, price, qty)
900	```
901	> **Why it wins:** `min` of the two remainings is exactly the largest fill both can honor — that IS the partial fill. The resting price is the price-time rule. One fill, one immutable trade — clean and correct.
902	
903	```python copy
904	    def _rest(self, order: Order) -> None:
905	        # WHY: negate price for bids so the MAX price sits on the heap top;
906	        #      asks use natural MIN. Sequence is the FIFO tie-break at one price.
907	        if order.side is Side.BUY:
908	            heapq.heappush(self._bids, _HeapEntry((-order.price_cents, order.sequence), order))
909	        else:
910	            heapq.heappush(self._asks, _HeapEntry((order.price_cents, order.sequence), order))
911	
912	    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
913	        order = self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
914	        if order is None:
915	            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found on {self.symbol}")
916	        if not order.is_active:
917	            raise OrderNotCancellableError(f"order {order_id} is {order.status.value}, cannot cancel")
918	        order.cancel()
919	        return order
920	
921	    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order | None:
922	        return self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)
923	
924	    def is_crossed(self) -> bool:
925	        # WHY: a healthy book is NEVER crossed after matching settles.
926	        bid, ask = self.best_bid(), self.best_ask()
927	        if bid is None or ask is None:
928	            return False
929	        return bid.price_cents >= ask.price_cents
930	
931	    def resting_quantity(self, side: Side) -> int:
932	        heap = self._bids if side is Side.BUY else self._asks
933	        return sum(e.order.remaining_qty for e in heap if e.order.is_active)
934	```

1134	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1135	
1136	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the race:** "It's in `submit_order`'s call to `book.match`. A single submit can sweep several resting orders and rest a remainder — that's many reads and writes to the book. Two submits on the SAME symbol at once can interleave: one reads a resting order's remaining quantity, the other does too, both fill it — phantom shares, or a half-swept book that's left crossed. It's a check-then-act race over the whole sweep, not a single field."
1137	
1138	🗣️ **Step 2 — Solution:** "Wrap the entire `match` call in a per-symbol lock. The whole sweep becomes one atomic step — a submit either fully resolves or doesn't run yet. I do it with the no-op `_lock` hook: the clean core yields nothing; the thread-safe subclass takes a real `RLock` for that symbol."
1139	
1140	🗣️ **Step 3 — Trade-off:** "Per-symbol, not global. Different symbols share no state, so AAPL and MSFT match completely in parallel; only same-symbol submits serialize, and a match is microseconds. One global lock would needlessly serialize every symbol through one bottleneck."
1141	
1142	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed:** "Across servers a Python lock protects only one process. I'd shard by symbol — each symbol's book lives on exactly one server (consistent hashing), so a symbol still has a single serialization point, just a network one. Cross-symbol scale is then just adding shards."
1143	
1144	**Concurrency Vocabulary**
1145	
1146	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1147	|---|---|---|
1148	| Race condition | Two threads touch shared state at once, result depends on timing | two submits sweeping the same book |
1149	| Critical section | The block that must run atomically | the whole `book.match` call |
1150	| Check-then-act | Check a condition, act — but state changed between | "saw 20 remaining, then filled 20" on two threads |
1151	| Atomic | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-symbol lock guarantees for a sweep |
1152	| Mutex / lock | A token only one thread holds | `threading.RLock` per symbol |
1153	| Reentrant (RLock) | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | future guarded helper calling another under the same symbol lock |
1154	| Lock granularity | How much one lock covers | per-SYMBOL, not one global |
1155	| Pessimistic locking | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process per-symbol RLock |
1156	| Optimistic locking | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on conflict | the DB compare-and-set for the distributed case |
1157	| Compare-and-set | Atomic "update only if value still X" | a sharded `UPDATE ... WHERE status='OPEN'` |
1158	| Deadlock | Two threads each hold what the other needs | avoided: we only ever hold ONE lock at a time |
1159	| Starvation | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk; locks are held microseconds |
1160	
1161	**Concurrency Follow-up Q&A**
1162	
1163	> ❓ "Why per-symbol, not one global lock?"
1164	> "Different symbols share zero state, so a global lock would serialize AAPL behind MSFT for no reason. Per-symbol locking lets every symbol match in parallel; only same-symbol orders take turns, and a match is microseconds. It's the natural granularity because the book is already partitioned by symbol."
1165	
1166	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1167	> "Defensive reentrancy: if a guarded method ever calls another method that also takes the same symbol's lock, a plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread. An `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only releases on the outermost exit. The cost over a plain `Lock` is negligible and it removes a whole class of future bugs."
1168	
1169	> ❓ "Why lock the whole `match` instead of just the field write?"
1170	> "Because the unit of correctness is the entire sweep, not one decrement. A single submit reads the best opposite order, fills it, reads the next, fills it, rests the remainder — if another thread sees the book between any two of those steps it can match against a half-filled order or leave the book crossed. Conservation is a property of the whole sweep, so the whole sweep is the critical section."
1171	
1172	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1173	> "None. A submit or cancel only ever holds ONE lock — the lock for its symbol — and never tries to grab a second. With no lock-ordering cycle there's no deadlock. The IO I'd add in production (publishing trades to a feed) stays OUTSIDE the lock."
1174	
1175	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1176	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and same-symbol contention is bounded, so a short-held lock is simpler than a retry loop. For the distributed case I'd flip to optimistic — a compare-and-set on a sharded store — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."

1191	        super().__init__(symbols)
1192	        self._symbol_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {
1193	            s: threading.RLock() for s in self._books     # one lock per symbol
1194	        }
1195

1197	    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
1198	        lock = self._symbol_locks.get(symbol)

Let me confirm with a more aggressive reproduction that injects a yield into the read path, matching the reviewer's described mechanism. But regardless of reproduction difficulty, the structural defect is real and the fix is clear: acquire the lock in the read path. Let me apply the fixes.

The cleanest fix that addresses the root cause is to make peeks non-mutating in read paths AND acquire the lock in `snapshot()`. The reviewer offers two options. The most robust approach: acquire the per-symbol lock in `snapshot()`. This is the minimal, correct fix that keeps `_peek_active`'s lazy-purge semantics (which are needed for matching efficiency) while ensuring no two threads mutate the same heap concurrently.

Let me apply fix #1 to `exchange_service.py` — wrap the book reads in `snapshot()` with the per-symbol lock.

def snapshot(self, symbol: str) -> dict[str, int | bool | None]:
        # WHY: a read-only view of one book's top of book + resting depth. Used by
        #      the demo and tests to prove the book is consistent (not crossed).
        #      This LOOKS read-only, but best_bid/best_ask/is_crossed call
        #      _peek_active, which heappop()s lazily-deleted dead tops — a MUTATION
        #      of the heap list. So a monitoring thread polling snapshot() races a
        #      submit_order() on the same symbol: two threads mutating one heap

# ── CONCURRENCY: the READ path (snapshot) races the WRITE path (submit) ─────────
#
# snapshot() looks read-only but isn't: best_bid/best_ask/is_crossed call
# _peek_active, which heappop()s lazily-deleted (cancelled/filled) dead tops — a
# MUTATION of the same heap list that match() pops from. A monitoring thread
# polling snapshot() therefore shares heap mutation with a submit on the SAME
# symbol. The two tests below (a) prove the shipped engine survives that race and
# (b) prove the per-symbol lock is what makes it survive — i.e. the lock is
# genuinely load-bearing, not decorative.


def _race_snapshot_against_submit(engine: MatchingEngine) -> list[str]:
    # Seed many resting SELLs, then cancel them so they become DEAD tops the
    # purge logic must pop. Crossing BUYs walk _asks popping dead tops in match(),
    # while pollers walk _asks popping the same dead tops in snapshot() -> two
    # threads mutating ONE heap. Returns any exceptions seen (a torn book).
    errors: list[str] = []

    ids: list[str] = []
    for i in range(400):
        order, _ = engine.submit_order(
            "AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000 + i % 3, quantity=10
        )
        ids.append(order.order_id)
    for order_id in ids:
        engine.cancel_order(order_id)

    stop = threading.Event()

    def poller() -> None:
        while not stop.is_set():
            try:
                engine.snapshot("AAPL")
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - the whole point is to catch a torn book
                errors.append(repr(exc))
                return

    def submitter() -> None:
        for _ in range(60):
            try:
                engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=2000, quantity=10)
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - a torn heap surfaces here too
                errors.append(repr(exc))
                return

    pollers = [threading.Thread(target=poller) for _ in range(6)]
    writer = threading.Thread(target=submitter)
    for p in pollers:
        p.start()
    writer.start()
    writer.join()
    stop.set()
    for p in pollers:
        p.join()

    return errors


def test_snapshot_concurrent_with_submit_does_not_tear_book() -> None:
    # The shipped, locked engine: polling snapshot() while submits flow on the
    # SAME symbol must NEVER tear the heap, because snapshot now takes the same
    # per-symbol lock. No exception, and the book is still consistent afterward.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(engine)

    assert errors == [], f"snapshot/submit race tore the book: {errors[:3]}"
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["crossed"] is False
    assert snap["ask_qty"] >= 0 and snap["bid_qty"] >= 0


def test_per_symbol_lock_is_load_bearing(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    # NEGATIVE CONTROL. We widen the check-then-act window in _peek_active (the
    # heap-mutating purge) with a yield so two threads can both pass the dead-top
    # check and both heappop. With this window open:
    #   - the no-op-lock MatchingEngine TEARS the heap (IndexError) — proving the
    #     race is real and the lock is the only thing preventing it;
    #   - the ThreadSafeMatchingEngine stays clean — proving the lock closes it.
    original_peek = OrderBook._peek_active

    def slow_peek(self: OrderBook, heap: list) -> object:
        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
            time.sleep(0)  # force a thread switch BETWEEN the check and the pop
            heapq.heappop(heap)
        return heap[0].order if heap else None

    monkeypatch.setattr(OrderBook, "_peek_active", slow_peek)
    try:
        # Unlocked core: the widened window must produce a torn book.
        unlocked_errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(MatchingEngine(["AAPL"]))
        assert unlocked_errors, (
            "expected the unlocked engine to tear under the widened race window; "
            "if this is empty the lock is not actually load-bearing"
        )

        # Same window, but the per-symbol lock serialises the heap mutations.
        locked_errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(
            ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
        )
        assert locked_errors == [], (
            f"the per-symbol lock failed to close the race: {locked_errors[:3]}"
        )
    finally:
        monkeypatch.setattr(OrderBook, "_peek_active", original_peek)

content.text:
Both MAJOR defects are fixed and re-verified.

Issue 1 (read path bypasses the lock while mutating the heap): `snapshot()` in `exchange_service.py` now wraps its book reads in `with self._lock(symbol):`, so `_peek_active`'s `heappop` of dead tops can no longer race a concurrent `submit_order` on the same symbol. The symbol-existence guard stays outside the lock (reads only the fixed symbol set), and `get_order` was left untouched since it is a pure `dict.get`. The matching §6 code block in `_response.md` was updated to match.

Issue 2 (test didn't prove the lock is load-bearing; bogus "390 remaining" narrative): I added two tests plus a shared driver in `tests/test_exchange.py`. One races `snapshot()` against `submit_order()` on one symbol and asserts the book never tears. The other is a negative control that widens the `_peek_active` check-then-act window with an injected yield and asserts the no-op-lock `MatchingEngine` reliably tears (`IndexError: index out of range` out of `heapq.heappop` — exactly the reviewer's reproduction), while `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` stays clean. I corrected the §7.2 / §9 narrative: test count 24→26, replaced the unreproducible "390 remaining / Order.fill lost-update" claim with the real, reproducible heap-tear control, and added an honest note that CPython's GIL closes the `fill` read-modify-write window.

Final status: `main.py` exits 0 (all 7 cases pass); `pytest -q` reports 26 passed (was 24), stable across repeated runs. No placeholder bodies, no bare excepts, sequence diagram untouched and semicolon-free.
